facts.statement: Lalibela, Ethiopia contains 11 monolithic churches carved from living rock, traditionally dated to King Lalibela's reign (c. 1181–1221).

variants.question: Local tradition says King Lalibela commissioned his rock-hewn churches partly in response to which Muslim military victory in 1187?
distractors: The Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Lalibela's rock-hewn church complex declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site?
facts.explanation: Lalibela's rock-hewn churches were declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1978. The site continues to be an active center of Ethiopian Orthodox Christian pilgrimage. Source: Wikipedia, Lalibela.
facts.wowFactor: Lalibela's churches have been in continuous liturgical use for 800 years — pilgrims still travel to them for Ethiopian Christmas and Easter, as they have since the 13th century.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a UNESCO heritage plaque mounted beside a Lalibela rock church entrance, Ethiopian pilgrims and international visitors approaching the excavated courtyard.
facts.statement: Lalibela's 11 rock-hewn churches were declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1978.

facts.quizQuestion: In approximately what year did the Almoravid dynasty found Marrakesh as their capital?
facts.explanation: The Almoravids founded Marrakesh as their capital c. 1070. They emerged in the 1050s from Sanhaja Berber tribes in present-day Mauritania and Western Sahara. Their name derives from Arabic 'al-Murabit,' connected to the concept of a ribat (frontier monastery-fortress). They were defeated by the Almohads in 1147. Source: Wikipedia, Almoravid dynasty.
facts.wowFactor: Marrakesh — now one of Morocco's most famous cities — was founded less than 1,000 years ago by Saharan Berber warriors who crossed the desert to build an empire. The city's name gives Morocco its name.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Almoravid Berber warriors in desert robes founding a settlement at an oasis, a modest town plan being laid out, the Atlas mountains in the background.
facts.statement: The Almoravids founded Marrakesh as their capital c. 1070; the city gives Morocco its name in many languages.

distractors: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa
facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the 1086 battle where the Almoravids achieved their signature victory, halting Christian Reconquista advances in Spain?
facts.explanation: The Battle of Sagrajas (1086) was the Almoravids' signature victory in Spain, halting Christian Reconquista advances. By 1090–1091, the Almoravids had absorbed major Spanish city-states: Granada, Córdoba, Seville, and Almería. The Almoravids also conquered the Ghana Empire around 1076–77, though scholars debate whether this was military conquest or gradual political influence. Source: Wikipedia, Almoravid dynasty.
facts.wowFactor: The Almoravids arrived from the Sahara to help Andalusian Muslims against Christian crusaders — then stayed and absorbed the kingdoms they came to help. Their 'rescue' was also a conquest.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Almoravid Berber cavalry in black robes and veils clashing with armored Castilian knights on a Spanish plain, Almoravid victory flags rising.
facts.statement: The Battle of Sagrajas (1086) was the Almoravids' decisive victory halting the Christian Reconquista in Spain.

facts.quizQuestion: Around what year did the Almoravids under Abu Bakr's leadership conquer or impose political influence over the Ghana Empire?
facts.explanation: The Almoravids under Abu Bakr conquered the Ghana Empire around 1076–77 — though scholars debate whether this was military conquest or gradual political influence. The Ghana Empire subsequently fell to the Sosso around 1203 and became a vassal of the rising Mali Empire by the mid-13th century. Source: Wikipedia, Ghana Empire / Almoravid dynasty.
facts.wowFactor: The Ghana Empire's fall to the Almoravids in 1076–77 is debated — some scholars think they conquered it militarily, others think Islamic conversion gradually undermined it. Medieval Africa's history is still being untangled.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Almoravid Berber warriors in veils entering Koumbi Saleh, the Ghana Empire capital, with local traders watching cautiously from doorways.
facts.statement: The Almoravids conquered or politically dominated the Ghana Empire around 1076–77, though scholars debate whether this was military or gradual influence.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Moroccan city served as the major northern terminus of trans-Saharan trade routes, where West African gold was struck into Almoravid dinars?
facts.explanation: Sijilmasa in Morocco served as 'a major northern terminus where gold was struck into Almoravid dinars.' It was the point where trans-Saharan gold caravans from West Africa entered the Mediterranean world. The triangular trade: salt from northern mines (especially Taghaza) was exchanged for gold in the south; gold moved north to Sijilmasa. Source: Wikipedia, Trans-Saharan trade.
facts.wowFactor: Sijilmasa processed West African gold into Islamic coins — the Almoravid dinar — that circulated from Morocco to Spain to the Middle East. A town in Morocco was the mint for most of medieval Europe's gold supply.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Sijilmasa's gold market: desert caravans arriving with leather pouches of gold dust, mint workers melting and stamping Almoravid dinars in a courtyard workshop.
facts.statement: Sijilmasa in Morocco was the major northern terminus of trans-Saharan trade, where West African gold was struck into Almoravid dinars.

facts.quizQuestion: What were the primary export goods of the Swahili Coast cities traded into the Indian Ocean network?
facts.explanation: The Swahili Coast exported 'timber, ivory, animal skins, spices, and gold.' Imports included Chinese silk and porcelain, and Indian spices and cotton. This Indian Ocean trade network connected East Africa to Arabia, India, Persia, and China. Kilwa, Mombasa, and Zanzibar were the major Swahili trade cities. Source: Wikipedia, Swahili coast.
facts.wowFactor: Swahili Coast ivory exported through Kilwa ended up carved into chess pieces, piano keys, and decorative objects from Persia to China — African elephants connected to Asian luxury goods markets.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Swahili coast warehouse with ivory tusks, bundles of animal skins, bark spice bundles, and sacks of gold dust arranged for loading onto Arab dhows.
facts.statement: Swahili Coast cities exported timber, ivory, animal skins, spices, and gold; they imported Chinese silk and porcelain and Indian cotton.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Sonni Ali annex Timbuktu for the Songhai Empire?
facts.explanation: Sonni Ali annexed Timbuktu in 1468, bringing the great center of Islamic learning under Songhai control. Askia Muhammad later reorganized the empire after seizing power in 1493. The Songhai Empire reached its peak around 1528, covering approximately 1.4 million km², before falling to Moroccan forces at the Battle of Tondibi in 1591. Source: Wikipedia, Songhai Empire.
facts.wowFactor: Songhai controlled Timbuktu for 123 years — long enough for the empire to become the guarantor of the most important intellectual center in sub-Saharan Africa.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Sonni Ali's Songhai cavalry entering Timbuktu in 1468, Islamic scholars watching nervously from their madrasah doorways, Songhai banners raised over the city.
facts.statement: Sonni Ali annexed Timbuktu for the Songhai Empire in 1468.

facts.quizQuestion: The Mali Empire lasted from approximately 1235 to what year — when Mahmud Keita IV's death led to fragmentation?
facts.explanation: The Mali Empire existed from approximately 1235 (Battle of Kirina) to approximately 1610 when Mahmud Keita IV's death led to fragmentation — a span of roughly 375 years. At its peak under Mansa Musa, it was one of the largest and wealthiest empires in the world. Source: Wikipedia, Mali Empire.
facts.wowFactor: The Mali Empire lasted 375 years — longer than the United States has existed — spanning from the death of the Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II to the early Baroque era in Europe.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a timeline scroll showing the Mali Empire's span from Sundiata Keita's battle standard to a fading empire split between rival claimants.
facts.statement: The Mali Empire lasted from c. 1235 to c. 1610 — roughly 375 years — finally fragmenting after Mahmud Keita IV's death.

facts.quizQuestion: How long did Mansa Musa remain in Cairo during his 1324 hajj — long enough to crash the city's gold market?
facts.explanation: Mansa Musa arrived at Cairo's outskirts in July 1324, crossing the Nile on July 19, and remained in Cairo for three months, departing October 18. During this time, his gold spending caused gold prices in Egypt to drop from 25 to under 22 silver dirhams — a depreciation that 'lasted at least 12 years.' Source: Wikipedia, Mansa Musa.
facts.wowFactor: Three months was all it took for Mansa Musa to collapse Egypt's precious metals market. He spent and gave away gold so freely that Cairo's economy was still recovering 12 years later.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Mansa Musa's entourage camped outside Cairo's walls for three months, gold flowing out in every direction — gifts, alms, market purchases — Egyptian merchants overwhelmed.
facts.statement: Mansa Musa stayed three months in Cairo (July–October 1324), spending enough gold to crash Egypt's precious metals market for at least 12 years.
